segments.text: Here's the expanded script,
Approx 2450 words, 16 to 17 minutes.
Hey, it's Friday.
Welcome to Tesla Short's Time Daily, episode 413
from March 20th, 2026.
I'm Patrick coming to you from a gray,
but not too cold Vancouver.
Let's wrap up the week in Tesla Land
and talk about what actually matters.
We'll start with the regulatory side
because it's been the biggest headline this week.
NHTSA has officially upgraded its investigation
into how Tesla's full self-driving system
handles glare and low visibility conditions.
What started as a more limited look
has now become a full engineering analysis,
and it now covers roughly 3.2 million vehicles on the road.
The agency is zeroing in on two things.
How the system performs when cameras are dealing
with bright sunlight, reflections, or heavy rain and fog,
and whether the car's degradation detection
is doing a good enough job of noticing
when its visibility is compromised
and clearly telling the driver to take over.
This escalation is significant
because it moves the review from a preliminary evaluation
into something much more serious.
A finding that the system isn't performing adequately
in these conditions could lead to an over-the-air recall
or mandated software changes.
From my perspective, this feels like a legitimate
engineering and regulatory challenge
rather than just political theater.
Camera-only systems do have physical limits
when the lenses get overwhelmed by glare
or the image gets washed out.
The neural nets are working with degraded data.
The question is whether Tesla's software is honest enough
with the driver in those moments.
Tesla will now have to hand over
a lot more detailed performance data,
crash logs, and internal testing results.
It adds another layer of oversight
to the supervised autonomy program
and it matters for both the company's relationship
with safety regulators and for everyday owners'
confidence in the system.
That said, even as the regulators tighten the screws,
Tesla continues to push the software forward
at its usual pace.
Elon Musk has said that FSD version 14.3
is already in internal testing
and should start rolling out to employees
in the next few weeks.
The update was originally hoped for back in January
or February, but like a lot of these releases,
it took a little longer than planned.
The main focus this time around
is on improved reasoning and logic,
especially around navigation decisions
and those why did it do that,
at moments that still frustrate a lot of owners.
This is important because navigation and decision-making
have been two of the most consistent complaints I hear
from people running the current versions.
If 14.3 actually delivers noticeably smarter behavior
in complex intersections, highway merges,
and unfamiliar routes,
it could be one of those updates
that owners feel in their daily driving.
According to Tesla Ratie,
the update is specifically targeting
some of those longstanding navigation gripes.
Even with the regulatory pressure mounting,
the team at Tesla is still iterating quickly
on the supervised system.
That rapid improvement cycle remains
one of the core advantages of their approach,
and it's going to be interesting
to see how much smarter the cars feel
once this version gets into more hands.
While the autonomy story is getting more complicated
on the regulatory front,
the charging business just took a meaningful step forward.
As of yesterday, Stellantis vehicles
that's Jeep, Dodge, Ram, Fiat, and Maserati
can now use Tesla's Supercharger network
across North America.
This completes the alignment
with the last major legacy automaker group up here
and it opens more than 27,500 Supercharger stalls
to a much wider group of EV owners.
For people driving non-Tesla vehicles,
this is a pretty big deal for real world convenience.
One of the biggest barriers to buying an EV
has always been the worry about finding a reliable charger
when you're on a longer trip.
Now a whole bunch more drivers can simply pull
into a Supercharger site,
use the Tesla app and get going again.
From Tesla's side,
this is about utilization and revenue.
More vehicles that can plug in
means higher usage rates on stalls
that might otherwise sit idle
during certain times of day.
According to TeslaNorth.com,
Stellantis was the final big North American holdout
so this feels like the end of one chapter
and the beginning of a more open charging ecosystem.
It's a good reminder that in the charging game,
the number of stalls you have is only part of the story.
What really matters is how often each stall
is actually being used
and how much revenue it generates.
And that brings us nicely to another story
that dropped this week that shows how seriously
other players are taking the next phase of EVs in autonomy.
Uber announced it will invest up to $1.25 billion
in Rivian to help build thousands of autonomous R2 robotaxies.
The plan is to start deploying them in 2028
across the US, Canada and Europe.
This isn't just another concept render.
It's a major ride hailing platform
teaming up with an EV maker
that's aiming for high volume, more affordable vehicles.
The partnership is targeting at least 10,000 vehicles
by the end of 2031.
What strikes me about this is how it creates
another serious contender in the autonomous ride hailing race.
It's not just Tesla anymore.
You've got traditional automakers, tech platforms
and now this Rivian Uber combination
pursuing scale through a different path.
It puts a bit more pressure on Tesla
to hit its own unsupervised robotaxie timeline.
Different companies are choosing different strategies.
Some are going heavy on sensors.
Others are betting on vision and software like Tesla.
The fact that multiple credible paths
are being funded shows this market is becoming very real.
It's going to be fascinating to watch
which approach actually scales safely and profitably first.
On a lighter note, there was another story this week
that shows how Tesla's cameras are delivering
practical value in the real world.
A woman in North Carolina was arrested
after sentry mode footage clearly captured
her vandalizing a cyber truck.
The owner was able to pull the video,
identify the suspect and hand it over to police.
This adds to a growing list of cases
where Tesla vehicles have helped solve vandalism
or break in attempts remarkably quickly.
I think these stories matter more than they might seem at first.
They give owners a tangible sense of security
that goes beyond just having an alarm.
When you know your car is always watching
and recording in high quality,
it changes how safe you feel leaving it parked somewhere.
According to Drive Tesla,
this incident is just the latest example
of how the built-in security features
are providing real world benefits.
These stories also travel fast in owner communities.
Every time one pops up, you see it shared in all the Tesla groups
and it reinforces the idea that these cars
are more than just transportation.
They're surprisingly capable digital guardians.
Shifting gears a bit,
there was some interesting movement
in the ultra-luxury segment this week.
Rolls-Royce has decided to abandon its plan
to go fully electric by 2030.
Instead, they'll continue building v12 models
for the foreseeable future
because adoption in the ultra-luxury space
has been slower than they expected.
This feels like a pretty honest admission
from a brand that knows its customers extremely well.
It highlights how differently the EV transition
is playing out across different price points.
What works in the mainstream market
doesn't always translate to the very top end,
at least not yet.
According to Drive Tesla,
this serves as a reminder that the luxury EV market
still faces pockets of resistance.
For Tesla, it's another data point
that not every segment is converting at the same pace.
While Cybertruck and the more accessible models
are finding strong demand,
the ultra-wealthy buyer who wants the theater
and sound of a v12 is still holding on.
It's a useful perspective
when we talk about the pace of the overall industry shift.
Closer to home for a lot of owners,
the Chattanooga charge event is coming up
and it looks like it's gonna be a good one.
There will be Cybertruck convoys, kid-friendly zones,
and a full slate of Tesla and EV community activities.
These gatherings have become a real part
of the Tesla ecosystem,
especially in certain regions of the country.
What I like about events like this
is that they keep the human side of the brand visible.
Yes, Tesla is a technology company
pushing hard on autonomy and energy,
but it's also built a passionate owner community
that likes getting together, showing off their cars
and talking about range, software updates, and road trips.
According to Tesla Rottie,
this is shaping up to be the Southeast's wildest Tesla party
yet.
In an era where so much of our interaction
with the brand happens through screens
and over-the-air updates,
these in-person events still matter.
They remind us that behind all the neural nets
and regulatory filings,
there are real people who are genuinely excited
about their vehicles.
Before we wrap up,
it's worth sitting with the regulatory pressure
on the FSD visibility side for a moment.
NHTSA's formal engineering analysis
is looking at whether the system properly detects
when its cameras can't see properly
and whether it gives drivers clear, timely warnings
to take over.
This isn't a trivial problem.
Camera-based systems have to be smart enough
to know when they're blind
and that's harder than it sounds
in rapidly changing conditions.
Tesla's approach has always been
to keep improving the software as fast as possible
using the massive amount of real-world data
the fleet generates.
That philosophy is being tested right now.
The outcome of this review could still force changes
or even a recall,
but these kinds of regulatory hurdles
are honestly part of bringing
any complex new technology into the mainstream.
How Tesla responds in the coming months
will tell us a lot about how ready the system
really is for wider adoption.
Taking a step back from the headlines
I keep coming back to the real economics
of public charging networks.
The surprising truth is that the value of a charging stall
isn't just in how many you build.
It's in how often each one is actually used
and how much revenue it generates every single day.
With Stellantis brands now able to plug in
the total number of vehicles
that can use the existing supercharger network
has grown significantly
without Tesla having to add a single new stall.
That's smart business.
Tesla has always treated the supercharger network
like any other engineering problem.
Measure the data, open idle capacity to more vehicles,
and only build where the utilization numbers justify it.
Opening the network to Jeep, Ram, Dodge,
and the rest turns previously idle stalls
into revenue generating assets.
It strengthens the economic case for further expansion
and quietly builds a more durable competitive advantage.
In the end, utilization and the business model
around charging may end up
mattering more than raw stall count.
All right, that's a wrap for today.
Keep an eye on how the NHTSA engineering analysis develops
over the next few weeks
and whether Tesla announces any software adjustments
in response.
TSLA closed today at $380.30,
down $12.92 or about 3.3%.
If you found this useful, a rating or review
on Apple podcasts or Spotify
really does help new listeners discover the show.
You can also find us on X at Tesla Short's Time.
I'm Patrick here in Vancouver.
Thanks for listening and I'll catch you again tomorrow.
Let me know if you'd like any sections tightened
or expanded further.
This podcast is curated by Patrick
but generated using AI voice synthesis of my voice
using 11 labs.
The primary reason to do this is I unfortunately don't have
the time to be consistent with generating all the content
and wanted to focus on creating consistent
and regular episodes for all the themes that I enjoy
and I hope others do as well.
